question:
Prove that there are unique positive integers $a$, $n$ such that
$a^{n+1}-(a+1)^n=2001$.
solution:
Suppose $a^{n+1} - (a+1)^n = 2001$.
Notice that $a^{n+1} + [(a+1)^n - 1]$ is a multiple of $a$; thus
$a$ divides $2002 = 2 \times 7 \times 11 \times 13$.

Since $2001$ is divisible by 3, we must have $a \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$,
otherwise one of $a^{n+1}$ and $(a+1)^n$ is a multiple of 3 and the
other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now
$a^{n+1} \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, so we must have $(a+1)^n \equiv 1
\pmod{3}$, which forces $n$ to be even, and in particular at least 2.

If $a$ is even, then $a^{n+1} - (a+1)^n \equiv -(a+1)^n \pmod{4}$.
Since $n$ is even, $-(a+1)^n \equiv -1 \pmod{4}$. Since $2001 \equiv 1
\pmod{4}$, this is impossible. Thus $a$ is odd, and so must divide
$1001 = 7 \times 11 \times 13$. Moreover, $a^{n+1} - (a+1)^n \equiv a
\pmod{4}$, so $a \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

Of the divisors of $7 \times 11 \times 13$, those congruent to 1 mod 3
are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both
congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus $a$ divides $7 \times 13$. Now
$a \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ is only possible if $a$ divides $13$.

We cannot have $a=1$, since $1 - 2^n \neq 2001$ for any $n$. Thus
the only possibility is $a = 13$. One easily checks that $a=13, n=2$ is a
solution; all that remains is to check that no other $n$ works. In fact,
if $n > 2$, then $13^{n+1} \equiv 2001 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$.
But $13^{n+1} \equiv 13 \pmod{8}$ since $n$ is even, contradiction.
Thus $a=13, n=2$ is the unique solution.

Note: once one has that $n$ is even, one can use that $2002
=a^{n+1} + 1 - (a+1)^n$ is divisible by $a+1$ to rule out cases.

descriptive_long.question:
Prove that there are unique positive integers $baseval$, $expterm$ such that
$baseval^{expterm+1}-(baseval+1)^{expterm}=2001$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Suppose $baseval^{expterm+1} - (baseval+1)^{expterm} = 2001$.
Notice that $baseval^{expterm+1} + [(baseval+1)^{expterm} - 1]$ is a multiple of $baseval$; thus
$baseval$ divides $2002 = 2 \times 7 \times 11 \times 13$.

Since $2001$ is divisible by 3, we must have $baseval \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$,
otherwise one of $baseval^{expterm+1}$ and $(baseval+1)^{expterm}$ is a multiple of 3 and the
other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now
$baseval^{expterm+1} \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, so we must have $(baseval+1)^{expterm} \equiv 1
\pmod{3}$, which forces $expterm$ to be even, and in particular at least 2.

If $baseval$ is even, then $baseval^{expterm+1} - (baseval+1)^{expterm} \equiv -(baseval+1)^{expterm} \pmod{4}$.
Since $expterm$ is even, $-(baseval+1)^{expterm} \equiv -1 \pmod{4}$. Since $2001 \equiv 1
\pmod{4}$, this is impossible. Thus $baseval$ is odd, and so must divide
$1001 = 7 \times 11 \times 13$. Moreover, $baseval^{expterm+1} - (baseval+1)^{expterm} \equiv baseval
\pmod{4}$, so $baseval \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

Of the divisors of $7 \times 11 \times 13$, those congruent to 1 mod 3
are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both
congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus $baseval$ divides $7 \times 13$. Now
$baseval \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ is only possible if $baseval$ divides $13$.

We cannot have $baseval=1$, since $1 - 2^{expterm} \neq 2001$ for any $expterm$. Thus
the only possibility is $baseval = 13$. One easily checks that $baseval=13, expterm=2$ is a
solution; all that remains is to check that no other $expterm$ works. In fact,
if $expterm > 2$, then $13^{expterm+1} \equiv 2001 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$.
But $13^{expterm+1} \equiv 13 \pmod{8}$ since $expterm$ is even, contradiction.
Thus $baseval=13, expterm=2$ is the unique solution.

Note: once one has that $expterm$ is even, one can use that $2002
=baseval^{expterm+1} + 1 - (baseval+1)^{expterm}$ is divisible by $baseval+1$ to rule out cases.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Prove that there are unique positive integers $lighthouse$, $sandcastle$ such that
$lighthouse^{sandcastle+1}-(lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle}=2001$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Suppose $lighthouse^{sandcastle+1} - (lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} = 2001$.
Notice that $lighthouse^{sandcastle+1} + [(lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} - 1]$ is a multiple of $lighthouse$; thus
$lighthouse$ divides $2002 = 2 \times 7 \times 11 \times 13$.

Since $2001$ is divisible by 3, we must have $lighthouse \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$,
otherwise one of $lighthouse^{sandcastle+1}$ and $(lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle}$ is a multiple of 3 and the
other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now
$lighthouse^{sandcastle+1} \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, so we must have $(lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} \equiv 1
\pmod{3}$, which forces $sandcastle$ to be even, and in particular at least 2.

If $lighthouse$ is even, then $lighthouse^{sandcastle+1} - (lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} \equiv -(lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} \pmod{4}$.
Since $sandcastle$ is even, $-(lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} \equiv -1 \pmod{4}$. Since $2001 \equiv 1
\pmod{4}$, this is impossible. Thus $lighthouse$ is odd, and so must divide
$1001 = 7 \times 11 \times 13$. Moreover, $lighthouse^{sandcastle+1} - (lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle} \equiv lighthouse
\pmod{4}$, so $lighthouse \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

Of the divisors of $7 \times 11 \times 13$, those congruent to 1 mod 3
are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both
congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus $lighthouse$ divides $7 \times 13$. Now
$lighthouse \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ is only possible if $lighthouse$ divides $13$.

We cannot have $lighthouse=1$, since $1 - 2^{sandcastle} \neq 2001$ for any $sandcastle$. Thus
the only possibility is $lighthouse = 13$. One easily checks that $lighthouse=13, sandcastle=2$ is a
solution; all that remains is to check that no other $sandcastle$ works. In fact,
if $sandcastle > 2$, then $13^{sandcastle+1} \equiv 2001 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$.
But $13^{sandcastle+1} \equiv 13 \pmod{8}$ since $sandcastle$ is even, contradiction.
Thus $lighthouse=13, sandcastle=2$ is the unique solution.

Note: once one has that $sandcastle$ is even, one can use that $2002
= lighthouse^{sandcastle+1} + 1 - (lighthouse+1)^{sandcastle}$ is divisible by $lighthouse+1$ to rule out cases.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Prove that there are unique positive integers $zeromagnitude$, $irrational$ such that
$zeromagnitude^{irrational+1}-(zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational}=2001$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Suppose $zeromagnitude^{irrational+1} - (zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} = 2001$.
Notice that $zeromagnitude^{irrational+1} + [(zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} - 1]$ is a multiple of $zeromagnitude$; thus
zeromagnitude divides $2002 = 2 \times 7 \times 11 \times 13$.

Since $2001$ is divisible by 3, we must have $zeromagnitude \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$,
otherwise one of $zeromagnitude^{irrational+1}$ and $(zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational}$ is a multiple of 3 and the
other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now
$zeromagnitude^{irrational+1} \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, so we must have $(zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} \equiv 1
\pmod{3}$, which forces $irrational$ to be even, and in particular at least 2.

If $zeromagnitude$ is even, then $zeromagnitude^{irrational+1} - (zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} \equiv -(zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} \pmod{4}$.
Since $irrational$ is even, $-(zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} \equiv -1 \pmod{4}$. Since $2001 \equiv 1
\pmod{4}$, this is impossible. Thus $zeromagnitude$ is odd, and so must divide
$1001 = 7 \times 11 \times 13$. Moreover, $zeromagnitude^{irrational+1} - (zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational} \equiv zeromagnitude
\pmod{4}$, so $zeromagnitude \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

Of the divisors of $7 \times 11 \times 13$, those congruent to 1 mod 3
are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both
congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus zeromagnitude divides $7 \times 13$. Now
$zeromagnitude \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ is only possible if zeromagnitude divides $13$.

We cannot have $zeromagnitude=1$, since $1 - 2^{irrational} \neq 2001$ for any $irrational$. Thus
the only possibility is $zeromagnitude = 13$. One easily checks that $zeromagnitude=13, irrational=2$ is a
solution; all that remains is to check that no other $irrational$ works. In fact,
if $irrational > 2$, then $13^{irrational+1} \equiv 2001 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$.
But $13^{irrational+1} \equiv 13 \pmod{8}$ since $irrational$ is even, contradiction.
Thus $zeromagnitude=13, irrational=2$ is the unique solution.

Note: once one has that $irrational$ is even, one can use that $2002
=zeromagnitude^{irrational+1} + 1 - (zeromagnitude+1)^{irrational}$ is divisible by $zeromagnitude+1$ to rule out cases.

garbled_string.question:
Prove that there are unique positive integers $qzxwvtnp$, $hjgrksla$ such that
$qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1}-(qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla}=2001$.
garbled_string.solution:
Suppose $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1} - (qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} = 2001$.
Notice that $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1} + [(qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} - 1]$ is a multiple of $qzxwvtnp$; thus
$qzxwvtnp$ divides $2002 = 2 \times 7 \times 11 \times 13$.

Since $2001$ is divisible by 3, we must have $qzxwvtnp \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$,
otherwise one of $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1}$ and $(qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla}$ is a multiple of 3 and the
other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now
$qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1} \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, so we must have $(qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} \equiv 1
\pmod{3}$, which forces $hjgrksla$ to be even, and in particular at least 2.

If $qzxwvtnp$ is even, then $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1} - (qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} \equiv -(qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} \pmod{4}$.
Since $hjgrksla$ is even, $-(qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} \equiv -1 \pmod{4}$. Since $2001 \equiv 1
\pmod{4}$, this is impossible. Thus $qzxwvtnp$ is odd, and so must divide
$1001 = 7 \times 11 \times 13$. Moreover, $qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1} - (qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla} \equiv qzxwvtnp
\pmod{4}$, so $qzxwvtnp \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.

Of the divisors of $7 \times 11 \times 13$, those congruent to 1 mod 3
are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both
congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus $qzxwvtnp$ divides $7 \times 13$. Now
$qzxwvtnp \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ is only possible if $qzxwvtnp$ divides $13$.

We cannot have $qzxwvtnp=1$, since $1 - 2^{hjgrksla} \neq 2001$ for any $hjgrksla$. Thus
the only possibility is $qzxwvtnp = 13$. One easily checks that $qzxwvtnp=13, hjgrksla=2$ is a
solution; all that remains is to check that no other $hjgrksla$ works. In fact,
if $hjgrksla > 2$, then $13^{hjgrksla+1} \equiv 2001 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$.
But $13^{hjgrksla+1} \equiv 13 \pmod{8}$ since $hjgrksla$ is even, contradiction.
Thus $qzxwvtnp=13, hjgrksla=2$ is the unique solution.

Note: once one has that $hjgrksla$ is even, one can use that $2002
=qzxwvtnp^{hjgrksla+1} + 1 - (qzxwvtnp+1)^{hjgrksla}$ is divisible by $qzxwvtnp+1$ to rule out cases.

kernel_variant.question:
Prove that there exist unique positive integers \(a\) and \(n\) satisfying
\[
a^{\,n+1}-(a+1)^{\,n}=223137.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  A first divisibility.
Add 1 to both sides:
\[
a^{n+1}+1-(a+1)^{n}=223137+1=223138.
\]
Because the left-hand side is clearly divisible by \(a\), we have
\[a\mid223138=2\cdot31\cdot59\cdot61.\]

Step 2.  Working modulo 3 - forcing \(a\equiv1\pmod3\) and an even \(n\).
Since \(223137\equiv0\pmod3\),
\[
a^{n+1}-(a+1)^{n}\equiv0\pmod3.
\]
Now
\[a^{n+1}\equiv1\pmod3\iff a\equiv1\pmod3,\]
for otherwise the two terms would differ in divisibility by 3.  Assuming
\(a\equiv1\pmod3\), we have \(a+1\equiv2\pmod3\) so that
\((a+1)^{n}\equiv2^{n}\pmod3.\)  For this to equal 1 modulo 3 we must have
\(n\) even.  In particular \(n\ge2\).

Step 3.  Working modulo 4 - ruling out even \(a\) and giving \(a\equiv1\pmod4\).
Because \(223137\equiv1\pmod4\), if \(a\) were even we would get
\[a^{n+1}\equiv0\pmod4,\qquad (a+1)^{n}\equiv1\pmod4\;(n\text{ even}),\]
so the difference would be \(-1\equiv3\pmod4\), impossible.  Thus \(a\) is odd
and still divides 223138; consequently
\[a\mid\tfrac{223138}{2}=111569=31\cdot59\cdot61\quad\text{and}\quad a\equiv1\pmod4.\]

Step 4.  Pinning down \(a\).
Among the odd divisors of 223138 the congruence requirements are
\(a\equiv1\pmod3\) and \(a\equiv1\pmod4\).  Inspecting the prime factors,
\[
31\equiv1\pmod3,\;31\equiv3\pmod4;\quad
59\equiv2\pmod3,\;59\equiv3\pmod4;\quad
61\equiv1\pmod3,\;61\equiv1\pmod4.
\]
The only prime factor meeting both conditions is 61, and any other odd
divisor contains 31 or 59, spoiling one of the congruences.  Hence \(a=61\).

Step 5.  Determining \(n\).
With \(a=61\) (so \(a\equiv5\pmod8\)) and knowing \(n\) is even, compare both
sides modulo 8.  Because \(223137\equiv1\pmod8\), we need
\[61^{n+1}-(62)^{n}\equiv1\pmod8.\]
Since \(62\equiv6\pmod8\), we have
\[
62^{n}=6^{n}\equiv
   \begin{cases}
   4,&n=2,\\
   0,&n\ge4\text{ even,}
   \end{cases}
\]
and \(61\equiv5\pmod8\) so that for odd exponent \(n+1\),
\[61^{n+1}\equiv5\pmod8.\]
Thus
\[
n=2\implies5-4\equiv1\pmod8\(\text{works}),\quad
n\ge4\text{ even}\implies5-0\equiv5\pmod8\(\text{fails}),
\]
and so \(n=2\).

Verification.
Finally,
\[61^{3}-62^{2}=226\,981-3\,844=223\,137,\]
so \((a,n)=(61,2)\) indeed satisfies the original equation.
Uniqueness follows from the modular restrictions above.

core_steps: Add 1 to the given equation to obtain an expression that is divisible by a, hence a | (2001+1).
Work mod 3 to force a ≡ 1 (mod 3) and deduce n is even.
Work mod 4 to rule out even a and obtain a ≡ 1 (mod 4); now a must be an odd divisor of (2001+1)/2.
Combine the congruence conditions with the prime-factorization of 2002 to isolate the single possible value of a.
Use mod 8 to show that the even exponent n must equal 2, giving the unique pair (a,n).

slot1.description: Right-hand side constant in the original equation.

slot2.description: Prime factorization of that constant plus 1, used to constrain a.

slot3.description: Small prime modulus that divides the constant and forces a ≡ 1 (mod p) and n even.

slot4.description: Parity modulus that excludes even a (needs the constant ≡ 1 (mod 4)).

slot5.description: Higher power-of-two modulus that rules out n > 2 once a is fixed.
